stories.title: Online tool connects farmers with available land to cattle ranchers in need of grazing area after wildfires
stories.summary: After Nebraska wildfires burned 800,000+ acres, the Nebraska Grazing Exchange launched a free online tool connecting ranchers who lost grazing land with farmers who have space available. The platform has drawn offers from people across the country, helping ranchers relocate entire herds and avoid economic collapse.
stories.extended_summary: Some ranchers lost 70-80% of their summer grazing range in a single wildfire. Now they're facing a choice: buy expensive hay by the truckload or move their entire herd to another state. The Nebraska Grazing Exchange stepped in with a free online tool that lets farmers offer available land and ranchers send a message to secure it. Offers poured in from all directions—north, south, east, and west—as neighbors rallied to help neighbors. The Nebraska Cattlemen's Association says the tragedy has brought out the best in the agricultural community, with many ranchers relocating hundreds of miles to keep their animals fed.

stories.title: Stocking up the Tiger's Table: Cram the Cruiser aims to support student snack pantries in local schools
stories.summary: What started as a school resource officer keeping chocolate on his desk for students has grown into "Cram the Cruiser," a community-wide food drive to stock snack pantries at two high schools. Officer Phil Anderson says the best part is being there when a student's having a rough day and helping them leave in a better place.
stories.extended_summary: Officer Phil Anderson started buying snacks out of his own pocket for students at Battle Ground High School. It was just a bowl of chocolate at first, but students kept coming back—not just for the food, but for someone to talk to. The pantry became a place where scared, angry, or frustrated kids could sit down and leave feeling better. Now the program has expanded to a second school, and on March 28, the community will fill police cruisers with non-perishable snacks at a Fred Meyer parking lot. Students even named the pantry themselves: the Tiger's Table. Anderson says those daily conversations—the chance to protect and serve by simply being present—are the most rewarding part of the job.

stories.title: MSU Reclaimed Project board member speaks about work in Southern Africa
stories.summary: MSU's Kappa Sigma fraternity raised over $1 million in two years for the Reclaimed Project, which serves vulnerable children in Lesotho, Africa, and Marks, Mississippi. More than 40 members spent Spring Break in Lesotho, where the nonprofit built a high school that's sending orphans to university in a country where fewer than 16% finish high school.
stories.extended_summary: In Lesotho, less than 16% of people have a high school education. But last year, Reclaimed Project's high school sent seven graduates to university in South Africa—and this year, they're on track to send even more. Jesse Carver, a board member, says it's surreal to hear an orphan in a Third-World country talk about plans for the future, about hopes and dreams. MSU's Kappa Sigma chapter has made Reclaimed Project their philanthropy, raising over $1 million through their annual Charity Bowl in just two years. Members do regular service at The Village educational center in Marks, Mississippi, and 40 of them traveled to Lesotho this Spring Break to work directly with the kids. Carver says once you see how much an education and a little attention can change a child's life, it's impossible not to keep showing up.

stories.title: Company that raised minimum salaries to $70,000 is still thriving
stories.summary: Nearly seven years after CEO Dan Price raised all employee salaries at Gravity Payments to a minimum of $70,000—taking a personal pay cut and selling his home to fund it—the company has doubled its workforce and thrived. Critics predicted bankruptcy, but lower turnover and skilled retention proved them wrong.
stories.extended_summary: When Dan Price announced he was raising every employee's salary to at least $70,000, critics lined up to predict his company's collapse. He slashed his own $1 million salary down to $70k, sold his second home, and braced for the fallout. That was nearly seven years ago. Gravity Payments didn't collapse—it doubled in size. Employees stayed longer, gained expertise, and served customers better. Turnover plummeted. Even after losing half its business during the pandemic, Gravity recovered and kept the $70k minimum intact. Price's gamble wasn't just moral—it turned out to be smart business.

It's part of a larger shift: millennials and Gen Z are pausing their relationship with alcohol, not necessarily quitting forever, just getting curious. young_achievers.extended_summary: Two Iranian filmmakers studying in Chicago turned their phones into archives during the 2022 Woman, Life, Freedom protests in Tehran. Their documentary short asks: what does revolution look like when it arrives through a screen? By the time protest footage reaches viewers outside Iran, it's been compressed, stripped of authorship, and pushed through social media feeds. What remains are fragments — someone running, a shaking hand at the edge of frame, a street seen through tempered glass. The film, which premiered at the 2026 Berlinale, doesn't try to package dissent into a single narrative. Instead, it examines how images circulate, detach, and take on new meaning as they move across borders and platforms.

young_achievers.extended_summary: Cloudy days have always been solar energy's weakness — until now. Filipino student Carvey Ehren Maigue created AuREUS, a material that converts UV radiation into visible light and then electricity, meaning it generates power even when clouds block direct sunlight. Since UV radiation penetrates cloud cover, the technology works significantly longer than conventional solar panels and doesn't need precise sun alignment. The material is made mainly from vegetable waste. In Nigeria, the Olowu of Owu is putting solar innovation into practice differently: his palace runs entirely on solar energy, with surplus electricity distributed to power street lighting and health centers. He's also using 3D-printed cowry shell tokens tracked via cloud technology to ensure over 5,000 schoolchildren receive their meals.

impact_innovation.title: Route-planning AI cut climate-warming contrails on over 100 flights
impact_innovation.summary: Google researchers tested AI-planned flight routes on 2,400+ real commercial flights between the US and Europe, successfully reducing contrails—the condensation streaks that cause more warming than aircraft CO2 emissions. The AI predicted ice-rich atmospheric regions and rerouted planes around them, proving the technology works at commercial scale for the first time.
impact_innovation.extended_summary: Those white streaks trailing behind jets? They trap more heat than the carbon dioxide planes emit. Google's AI learned to predict which patches of sky would spawn contrails using detailed weather forecasts, then recommended alternate routes to American Airlines pilots during 17 weeks of eastbound transatlantic flights. Over 2,400 flights participated in the randomized trial from January to May 2025, and the system worked—planes following AI guidance produced measurably fewer contrails. Earlier small-scale tests hinted this was possible, but this marks the first time contrail-reduction tech has been deployed across real commercial aviation at scale, not just in controlled experiments.

The program proved that removing one barrier could unlock housing stability for hundreds. Yet despite measurably working, it now faces an expiration date, threatening to slam the door shut again on pet owners experiencing homelessness.

impact_innovation.title: Dozens in Haiti rely on savings groups to access credit where bank loans are unavailable
impact_innovation.summary: In Port-au-Prince, community savings groups called AVÈK provide small loans through collective savings, offering an alternative to banks for vulnerable Haitians. One member used a $3,846 loan to buy a delivery truck, generating daily income and eventually funding a neighborhood vocational school—though sustainability challenges remain amid Haiti's instability.
impact_innovation.extended_summary: Julmice Bastien borrowed 500,000 gourdes (about $3,846) from his AVÈK savings group and bought a box truck. He contracted it to delivery drivers, splitting the daily $23 revenue and eventually earning enough to operate a neighborhood vocational school. AVÈK groups were introduced after Haiti's 2010 earthquake to boost economic resilience where bank loans are unavailable. Members pool savings and grant small loans to each other for businesses and income-generating activities. The model has supported financial inclusion and entrepreneurship across Haiti, particularly for vulnerable populations locked out of traditional banking. But sustainability remains fragile: repayment risks, lack of loan guarantees, and Haiti's ongoing economic collapse and gang violence all threaten the system. Still, for members like Bastien, it's been a path to financial independence in a country with few alternatives.

impact_innovation.title: Wheat on Repeat
impact_innovation.summary: After 20 years of research, Kansas scientists created Kernza—a perennial wheat-like grain that produces multiple harvests from one plant without replanting. The crop slashes emissions, stores carbon in soil, and reduces fertilizer and machinery use. Its flavor? Surprisingly warm, with hints of cinnamon and nutmeg.
impact_innovation.extended_summary: Lee DeHaan tells you not to drown Kernza pasta in sauce—you need to taste it. And when you do, there's cinnamon, then nutmeg, an earthy warmth tied to the Kansas soil it came from. But the real breakthrough isn't the flavor. It's that this grain never needs replanting. Unlike wheat, rice, and corn (which feed half of humanity and require annual planting, fertilizers, herbicides, and gas-guzzling machinery), Kernza is perennial. One plant, multiple harvests, year after year. That means slashed emissions, carbon stored in deep root systems, and healthier soil. The Land Institute spent two decades developing it. Now it's commercially available, a tantalizing glimpse of climate-friendly agriculture that doesn't require farmers to start over every spring. The fusilli in front of you isn't just pasta—it's proof that a better way to grow food is possible.

Justin Lefebure, the Rotarian leading the project, puts it simply: 'If it saves just one life, it will be worth it to us.'

impact_innovation.title: Saving the shells: A project to rebuild Alabama’s oyster reefs
impact_innovation.summary: Alabama Extension is turning discarded oyster shells into ecosystem rebuilding tools, keeping them out of landfills and back in coastal waters. Oyster reefs filter water, provide marine habitat, and stabilize shorelines — and researchers are working with communities to restore these critical structures that support both Gulf Coast ecosystems and local seafood economies.
impact_innovation.extended_summary: 'Oyster shells don't belong in a landfill,' says P.J. Waters, a coastal fisheries specialist with Alabama Extension. Instead, they're ecosystem engineers — building reef structures that house fish, filter nutrients from the water, and protect shorelines. Every oyster filters phytoplankton and sends heavier particles sinking to the sea floor, improving water quality with each cycle. Despite their importance, Alabama's reefs have declined dramatically. Now, through research and community engagement, Extension researchers are reclaiming shells from restaurants and returning them to the water, rebuilding the foundation of Alabama's estuaries one shell at a time.

impact_innovation.title: The Digital Divide After Prison: How Cornell Is Reimagining Reentry Through Technology
impact_innovation.summary: Cornell's Criminal Justice and Employment Initiative is tackling a hidden barrier to reentry: digital literacy. When a man released after 20 years asked 'What is email?', researchers created Rézme, a platform helping formerly incarcerated job seekers navigate automated hiring systems designed to filter them out before a human ever sees their application.
impact_innovation.extended_summary: The man had been out of prison for exactly one day when he asked Jodi Anderson Jr. a question that stopped him cold: 'What is email?' After 20 years inside, the man had never used a smartphone, sent an email, or touched modern technology — yet was expected to apply for jobs, housing, and benefits entirely online. That moment drove Anderson, director of technological innovation at Cornell's School of Industrial and Labor Relations, to co-found Rézme, a platform designed to help people navigate automated hiring algorithms that reject applications before they reach human eyes. 'Technology created many of these barriers,' Anderson explains. 'So technology has to be part of the solution.' It's a recognition that digital access isn't just convenience — it's survival.

Open the first and third Saturdays each month, the pantry operates on a simple understanding: 'People love their pets, and want to provide for them. In some cases, a dog or cat is a person's only local family,' Wilson says.

impact_innovation.title: Teachers Move Beyond AI Basics to More Sophisticated Instructional Uses
impact_innovation.summary: The National Academy for AI Instruction is training 400,000 teachers to use AI for sophisticated instructional support, not just lesson templates. At a recent New York City session, 50 teachers learned to build 'agentic' AI tools that can stress-test lessons for gaps, refine teaching approaches, and support deeper learning — moving beyond basic automation to real pedagogical partnership.
impact_innovation.extended_summary: Jing Liang Guan doesn't just want AI to write a lesson plan — he wants it to challenge his thinking, spot content gaps, and help him become a better teacher over time. The Brooklyn Science and Engineering Academy teacher was among 50 educators at a March 18 training in New York City learning to develop 'agentic' AI: autonomous systems that handle complex, multi-step reasoning tasks. It's part of a five-year, $23 million partnership between the American Federation of Teachers and three major AI developers to train 400,000 teachers in sophisticated AI use. As AI adoption among teachers nearly doubled from 2024 to 2025, the focus is shifting from surface-level tasks to tools that genuinely support instruction — leveraging teachers' professional judgment rather than replacing it.
